topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The invitation felt like an elaborate, poorly disguised trap. I smoothed the lapel of my borrowed blazer, the fabric suddenly feeling too stiff against my skin. When I walked into the sterile, beige office, the air immediately felt thick, heavy with an unfamiliar kind of quiet. And then I saw him. He was seated behind a mahogany desk, not the snarling figure from my youth, but impeccably dressed, his posture radiating a calm authority that made my jaw tighten. He looked up, and for a split second, I saw the familiar sharpness in his eyes, quickly masked by a practiced, gentle expression. "Welcome," he said, his voice low and smooth, entirely lacking the abrasive edge I remembered so well. I shifted my weight, my gaze sweeping over the room, cataloging every detail, searching for the telltale sign of the old tormentor. I found none, only this polished veneer. "I suppose you're here for an intake session." My tone was deliberately flat, a thin shield against the sudden, unwelcome flicker of old resentment that pricked at my throat.

[story 2]

I hated the forced pleasantries. Every time he offered a measured smile, I felt a tightening in my chest, a physical reaction that made me want to bolt. He was sitting across from me, leaning forward just slightly, his hands folded neatly on the desk. He spoke about trauma and coping mechanisms with an easy, almost hypnotic cadence. I kept my expression carefully neutral, focusing intently on a spot just above his left ear. Internally, a hot wave of irritation washed over me; this entire performance felt like a mockery of the antagonism we once shared. When he paused, waiting for my response, I let a deliberate silence stretch, letting the awkwardness hang between us like a bad smell. "So," I managed, my voice clipped and deliberately devoid of warmth, "what exactly do you expect from me?" The question was barbed, aimed directly at the facade, hoping to see the cracks beneath the practiced composure.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the clinic hummed, an irritating, persistent drone that grated on my nerves. I sat on the edge of the leather chair, my arms crossed tightly over my chest, a stance that communicated absolute disinterest. He was reviewing some notes, tapping a pen against a file, his focus absolute. It was unnerving to watch him operate in this new space—so controlled, so measured. I felt a distinct hardening around my eyes, a refusal to let any vulnerability show. When he finally looked up, his gaze was steady, assessing. "It takes courage to seek help," he remarked, the words dripping with a sympathy that felt utterly undeserved. I let a slow, deliberate breath escape, tasting the stale air. "Courage," I echoed, my voice carrying a cool, hard edge that I made sure he heard clearly, "is often just the willingness to admit you were wrong." I watched his reaction closely, waiting to see if the old fire could ignite, or if this new professionalism would extinguish it entirely.
